title: The Boss Over The Board
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When heן¿½s over a rough and unpopular shed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With the sins of the bank and the men on his head;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When he musnן¿½t look black or indulge in a grin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And thirty or forty men hate him like Sin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I am moved to admit, when the total is scored,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That itן¿½s just a bit off for the Boss-of -the-board.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I have battled a lot,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But my dreamן¿½s never soared
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the lonely position of Boss-of-the-board.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas a black-listed shed down the Darling: the Boss
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was a small man to see, though a big man to cross,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We had nought to complain of, except what we thought,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the Boss didnן¿½t boss any more than he ought;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the Union was booming, and Brotherhood soared,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So we hated like poison the Boss-of-the-board.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We could tolerate ן¿½handsן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We respected the cook;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the name of a Boss was a blot in our book.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½d a row with Big Duggan, a rough sort of Jim,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or, rather, Jim Duggan was ן¿½laying forן¿½ him!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His hate of Injustice and Greed was so deep
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That his shearing grew rough, and he ill-used the sheep.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I fancied that Duggan his manliness lowerן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When he took off his shirt to the Boss-of-the-board,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For the Boss was ten stone,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the shearer full-grown,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he might have, they said, let the crawler alone.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Though some of us there wished the fight to the strong,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet we knew in our hearts that the shearer was wrong.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the crawler was plucky, it canן¿½t be denied,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For he had to fight Freedom and Justice beside,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But he came up so gamely, as often as floored,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That a blackleg stood up for the Boss-of-the-board!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the fight was a sight,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And we pondered that night,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Itן¿½s surprising how some of those blacklegs can fight!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Next day at the office, when sadly the wreck
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Jim Duggan came up like a lamb for his cheque,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Said the Boss, ן¿½Donן¿½t be childish! Itן¿½s all past and gone;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I am short of good shearers. Youן¿½d better stay on.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And we fancied Jim Duggan our dignity lowerן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When he stopped to oblige a damned Boss-of-the-board.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We said nothing to Jim,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For a joke might be grim,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the subject, we saw, was distasteful to him.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Boss just went on as heן¿½d done from the first,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he favoured Big Duggan no more than the worst;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And when weן¿½d cut out and the steamer came down,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With the hawkers and spielers, to take us to town,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And weן¿½d all got aboard, ן¿½twas Jim Duggan, good Lord!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who yelled for three cheers for the Boss-of-the-board.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas a bit off, no doubt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And with Freedom about,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But a lot is forgot when a shed is cut out.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With Freedom of Contract maintained in his shed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the curse of the Children of Light on his head,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½s apt to long sadly for sweetheart or wife,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And his views be inclined to the dark side of life.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Truth must be spread and the Cause must be shored,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But itן¿½s just a bit rough on the Boss-of-the-board.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I am all for the Right,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But perhaps (out of sight)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As a son or a husband or father heן¿½s white.
meaning:
"The Boss Over The Board" is a quintessential example of Henry Lawson's signature style...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
